FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study baseline data (2016-2018), 9-10 year olds, 21 sites nationally distributed

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using propensity score matching and generalized linear models; cross-sectional baseline design

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Frontiers in Pediatrics

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time variables isolated by modality (passive viewing, video games, social media/video chat, texting); obesity (individual-trait-level health outcome) is DV; measured via Youth Screen Time Survey with separate categories

FT_IC3.evidence: Inferential analyses comparing autism vs. typically developing children on screen time (Table 2: ratio of means with 95% CI); obesity analysis by screen time category (Tables 3-5)

FT_IC4.evidence: Prevalence ratios reported with 95% CI and p-values across all screen time modalities (passive p=0.002, video games p=0.05, texting p=0.04)

FT_EC7_reason: Design is cross-sectional baseline; EC7 requires longitudinal design
notes: Screen time measured in multiple separate categories qualifying FT-IC2; obesity as individual-trait health outcome; no SM-as-DV-only design

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study baseline (9-11 years, n=8324) and year 2 follow-up (n=3891) from NDA release 2.0

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; longitudinal mediation design with resting-state fMRI and behavioral measures

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Developmental Cognitive Neuroscience

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen-Activity Proportion (SAP) score measuring screen exposure relative to non-screen activities; paired with fronto-striatal connectivity and behavioral inhibitory control measures (neural/behavioral individual-trait-level); modalities include TV/movies, video games, social media measured separately

FT_IC3.evidence: Longitudinal correlations: baseline r=-0.040 (95% CI -0.0605 to -0.0186, BF10=13.10); year 2 r=-0.0933 (95% CI -0.1231 to -0.0635, BF10=428995.74); group comparisons (t-tests); mediation path coefficients

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple quantitative results: correlation coefficients with bootstrapped confidence intervals, Bayesian factors, indirect effects (B=-6.920e-5, 95% CI -1.006e-4 to -4.303e-5), Cohen's d=0.427 for group effects

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is IV throughout (predicts neural connectivity and behavioral outcomes); longitudinal design but SM not DV-only
notes: Mediation analysis shows screen time mediates reward sensitivity effect on inhibitory control; multiple neural and behavioral measures qualify as individual-trait-level outcomes

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study baseline 2016-2018 (9-11 year olds, n=11,633) with 2-year follow-up data from 2018-2020

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; prospective cohort logistic regression design

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Preventive Medicine

FT_IC2.evidence: Qualifying screen time variables: total screen time, texting, video chatting, watching videos, playing video games (all reported separately); paired with suicidal behaviors (psychiatric/mental health outcome—individual-trait-level DV); measured via ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey

FT_IC3.evidence: Logistic regression with prospective associations: each additional hour total screen time aOR 1.09 (95% CI 1.03-1.14); texting aOR 1.36, video chat aOR 1.30, watching videos aOR 1.21, video games aOR 1.18

FT_IC4.evidence: Adjusted odds ratios and 95% confidence intervals reported for all screen time modalities with p-values <0.05 (Table 2)

FT_EC7_reason: Longitudinal design (baseline predicts 2-year outcomes). Screen time is IV in all analyses (predictor). Suicidal behaviors is DV. No analysis with screen/SM as IV examining other outcomes separately. Meets all three EC7 criteria
notes: Exclusion code FT-EC7: Longitudinal DV-only design. Screen modalities isolated and measured separately; suicidal behaviors assessed via KSADS-5 DSM-5 criteria
